questions.title: Animal Moral Weights
questions.heading: How much moral weight do you give to the welfare of animals compared to humans?
questions.info: Moral weights determine how much you value improvements to different types of beings. Human life-years are the baseline (1.0). Your answer here affects how the model scores funds that focus on animal welfare.

options.label: Only humans matter
options.description: Animal welfare is not counted. Only effects on humans are included in fund scores.

options.label: Animals matter, but much less than humans
options.description: Gives animals meaningful but low moral weight. Reflects substantial scepticism about the depth of animal experience relative to human experience.

options.label: Animals matter, but less than humans (RP starting point)
options.description: Moral weights based on research done in the moral weights project, giving animals meaningful but not equal weight to humans.

options.label: All sentient beings count equally
options.description: A year of suffering counts the same regardless of species. Strong moral egalitarianism across all sentient life.

questions.title: Time Discounting
questions.heading: How much do you care about effects that happen in the future, compared to effects happening now?
questions.info:
You might have reasons to discount effects that happen in the future. For example, the further out an effect is, the greater the chance that something intervenes and the estimated effect never occurs.

The model divides effects into six time periods: 0–5 years, 5–10 years, 10–20 years, 20–100 years, 100–500 years, and 500+ years. Each option sets a multiplier for each period. A multiplier of 0 means effects in that period are ignored entirely; 1 means they count fully.

options.label: All time periods count equally
options.description: Effects are valued the same regardless of when they occur. No discounting applied.

options.label: All time periods matter, but the near term gets more weight (RP starting point)
options.description: Near-term effects count fully. Effects further out are progressively discounted, with distant effects counting very little.

options.label: Only the next century matters, with more weight on the near term
options.description: Effects within the next century count, with more weight on the near term. Effects beyond that are ignored.

options.label: Near term only
options.description: Only effects in the next decade matter. Everything beyond that is ignored.

questions.title: Non-AI X-Risk Discount
questions.heading: Do you want to apply an extra discount to the scores of interventions that do not work on reducing existential risks from AI?
questions.info: This adjustment applies only to interventions that do not focus on reducing existential risk from AI. Global health and development, animal welfare, biosecurity, and nuclear risk interventions are reduced in value. The value is a discount fraction: 0.0 means no discount, 0.4 means a 40% discount, 1.0 means that all non-AI interventions score zero.

options.label: No discount
options.description: Non-AI existential risk interventions are evaluated the same way as all other interventions.

options.label: Small discount (10%)
options.description: Non-AI x-risk interventions are scored at 90% of their otherwise calculated value.

options.label: Large discount (40%) (RP starting point)
options.description: Non-AI interventions are scored at 60% of their otherwise calculated value.

options.label: Very large discount (80% discount)
options.description: Non-AI interventions are scored at 20% of their otherwise calculated value.

questions.title: Risk Attitude
questions.heading: How do you want to handle uncertainty and risk when comparing funds?
questions.info: Each fund's impact is estimated as an expected value across a range of scenarios with different probabilities. The risk profile determines how those scenarios are weighted. A risk-neutral approach takes expected value at face value. Other options adjust for uncertainty by discounting optimistic outliers, putting extra weight on potential harms, or both.

options.label: Risk neutral
options.description: Take expected value estimates at face value. If something has a 10% chance of saving 1,000 lives, count it as 100 expected lives saved. No adjustment for uncertainty or risk.

options.label: Skeptical of large upsides
options.description: Puts lower weight on the most optimistic scenarios. If a model says there's a small chance of enormous impact, this heavily discounts that claim.

options.label: Extra weight on potential harms
options.description: Put extra weight on avoiding harms. Scenarios where an intervention backfires are weighted 5x more heavily than equivalent positive outcomes.

options.label: Cautious across the board (RP starting point)
options.description: Penalizes high-variance outcomes: less impressed by very large claimed benefits and more concerned about very large potential harms. RP's recommended approach.
